Ok, you are right, I guess in the literature they use the term "low-carbon" for solar, wind, and hydro, and even though I think that is stupid and misleading, if that is the convention in the sources, we need to follow that.  As long as we note that hydro is comparable to nuclear, the readers of this article should easily understand that this "low-carbon" must have something to do with total lifecycle, and not generation emissions.

/* top */ nuclear is generally considered low-carbon. No source is zero carbon. Even if we are talking about generation only (which is a bit misleading) nuclear fuel require uranium mining and processing which produce some carbon emissions
[[Wikipedia:OABOT|Open access bot]]: doi updated in citation with #oabot.

Nukes emit NO carbon (dioxide) emissions.  They emit small amounts of tritium, which is impossible to completely contain.  Carbon emissions which happen when manufacturing steel or when manufacturing solar panels (as happens in China) are not counted because it is not involved in the generation.  We could completely decarbonize our economy and this would not require any changes to nuclear power plants.
